title: Representations, features, and concepts
learningObjectives: Explain representations, features, and concepts in plain language
Write down the objective and identify its assumptions
Design one test that could falsify an over-strong interpretation

whyThisLesson.text: A representation is a vector state; a feature is a direction or function that detects a recurring property; a concept is the human-level hypothesis attached to that feature. This lesson gives you a concrete test for that claim before the course moves to the next abstraction.

intuition.analogy: A map can store the same city with coordinates, pixels, or roads. A representation is the encoding; a feature is a useful direction through that encoding.
intuition.analogyLimit: A label is an interpretation of a pattern until interventions show that changing the feature changes the target behavior.
intuition.text: A representation is a vector state; a feature is a direction or function that detects a recurring property; a concept is the human-level hypothesis attached to that feature.

toyExample.description: Track one token through two contexts and compare its activation at the same layer. The changing vector is the object the later lessons will analyze.

steps.result: Specify exactly what representations, features, and concepts should recover, preserve, or ignore.
steps.insight: The intended factor must be known before a toy result can be judged.

steps.result: Use the same data, capacity, training budget, and evaluation examples for both methods.
steps.insight: Otherwise a visible gain may be a resource difference in disguise.

steps.result: Vary the source correlation, noise, view quality, or sparsity and record the first failure.
steps.insight: The boundary of success is usually more useful than the headline score.

visual.question: Which assumption behind representations, features, and concepts changes the result most?
visual.description: Move one control at a time. Treat the display as a toy model, then write down the real experiment it suggests.

technical.text: A representation is a vector state; a feature is a direction or function that detects a recurring property; a concept is the human-level hypothesis attached to that feature. In practice, write down the input unit, the operation applied to it, and the exact statistic used to score the result. Those are different things. A learned representation can be useful even when the interpretation attached to it turns out to be wrong.

mathematics.explanation: For this lesson, the expression makes the central trade-off explicit. A representation is a vector state; a feature is a direction or function that detects a recurring property; a concept is the human-level hypothesis attached to that feature.
mathematics.whyNeeded: It says precisely what the method is being rewarded for.
mathematics.ifRemoved: You could not tell whether two implementations were solving the same problem.
mathematics.encourages: A testable prediction about the chosen objective.
mathematics.doesNotGuarantee: A diagram of activations makes the pipeline visible, but it does not identify a concept or a mechanism.

methodComparison.question: What is being named?
methodComparison.practicalImplication: Keep the measurement separate from the interpretation.

evidence.claim: A representation is a vector state; a feature is a direction or function that detects a recurring property; a concept is the human-level hypothesis attached to that feature.
evidence.note: Course explanation. Use the research library's primary sources for paper-specific empirical claims.

failureModes.name: A score that answers the wrong question
failureModes.mechanism: The objective rewards a proxy while the paper describes a stronger scientific property.
failureModes.symptom: The headline metric rises, but seed stability, controls, or interventions disagree.
failureModes.diagnosticTest: Add a matched negative control and one counterfactual intervention.
failureModes.mitigation: Either narrow the claim or add the missing measurement.
failureModes.setconcaRelevance: Report both the intended benefit and the most plausible shortcut for every SetConCA loss term.

setconcaConnection.relevance: Representations, features, and concepts determines how SetConCA should represent, group, or evaluate activation views.
setconcaConnection.whichPart: The choice of sparse code, view set, coordination loss, or evaluation.
setconcaConnection.whichDesignChoice: Match the pointwise SAE and SetConCA on model, activation site, width, sparsity budget, and compute.
setconcaConnection.whichFailure: Mistaking stronger agreement for recovery of the intended concept.
setconcaConnection.whichExperiment: Compare fidelity, feature stability, selectivity, and controlled interventions; report each result separately.
setconcaConnection.alternativeExplanation: An apparent gain may come from extra context, effective capacity, or easier positives rather than set coordination.

exercises.prompt: A paper claims that representations, features, and concepts improved interpretability. What one result would you ask for before accepting that claim?
exercises.reveal: Ask for the smallest missing control: a matched baseline, several seeds, a negative control, or an intervention that tests the claimed mechanism.

masteryQuestions.prompt: Explain representations, features, and concepts to a colleague, then name one thing the method cannot establish by itself.

nextLesson.bridge: Next: Why neurons are not concepts. The next idea reuses this lesson's representation and makes a new assumption explicit.

fields.label: Falsifiable claim
fields.prompt: Write the smallest claim this lesson supports.

fields.label: Control
fields.prompt: What control would rule out a shortcut?
